covered_sections: 1.1 Description of the concept set>1.1.1 Set Description
1.2 The Notion of Sets>Finite and Infinite Sets
1.3 Operations on Sets>Symmetric Difference (A Δ B)

1.3 Operations on Sets>Difference (A − B: elements in A not in B

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The symmetric difference A Δ B is defined as (A \ B) ∪ (B \ A). Which of the following correctly describes A Δ B?

options.A: Elements in both A and B
options.B: Elements in A or B but not both
options.C: Elements in A but not in B
options.D: Elements in B but not in A

questions.explanation: A Δ B = (A \ B) ∪ (B \ A) consists of elements that are in A or B but not in both. Option B correctly states this as 'elements in A or B but not both'. Option A is intersection, C is A−B, D is B−A.

explanations_tiered.definition: Symmetric difference A Δ B = (A − B) ∪ (B − A) = elements that are in exactly one of A or B.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Symmetric difference excludes the intersection. It contains elements that belong to A or B, but not both simultaneously.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Symmetric difference is like 'either but not both'. It keeps everything from both sets except what they share.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A Δ B = (A ∪ B) − (A ∩ B). Elements must be in A or B, but cannot be in both. This is the XOR (exclusive or) operation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Symmetric difference is commutative (A Δ B = B Δ A) and associative. It corresponds to addition modulo 2 in characteristic 2 vector spaces.
reasoning_steps: Recall: A Δ B = (A − B) ∪ (B − A).
A − B = elements in A only.
B − A = elements in B only.
Union of these = elements in exactly one set.
misconceptions: Some confuse symmetric difference with union (includes intersection).
Some think A Δ B = A ∪ B, which is incorrect because symmetric difference excludes common elements.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: A Δ B = elements in A or B, not both.

glossary_entry.definition: The set of elements that belong to exactly one of the sets A or B, denoted A Δ B.

flashcard.front: What does A Δ B contain?
flashcard.back: Elements that are in A or B but not in both (exclusive or).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What operation gives elements in exactly one of two sets?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set of elements belonging to exactly one of A or B.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Venn diagram shading to show symmetric difference as the two exclusive regions.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define symmetric difference of two sets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A Δ B = (A\B) ∪ (B\A) = elements in exactly one set, i.e., A or B but not both.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If A = {1,2,3,4,5} and B = {4,5,6,7,8}, what is A Δ B?
questions.hint: A Δ B = (A−B) ∪ (B−A) = elements in exactly one set.
questions.explanation: A − B = {1,2,3}, B − A = {6,7,8}. Their union is {1,2,3,6,7,8}. Option C is correct. Option A is intersection, B is union, D is union incorrectly formatted.

explanations_tiered.definition: A Δ B = (A − B) ∪ (B − A). Elements that are in A or B but not in both.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A − B = {1,2,3}, B − A = {6,7,8}. Their union = {1,2,3,6,7,8}, which excludes the common elements 4 and 5.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Take A and B. Keep the numbers that are only in A (1,2,3) and only in B (6,7,8). Remove the numbers in both (4,5).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A Δ B = {1,2,3,4,5} Δ {4,5,6,7,8} = {1,2,3,6,7,8}. The elements 4 and 5 appear in both, so they are excluded.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Symmetric difference corresponds to XOR. The characteristic function satisfies χ_{A Δ B} = χ_A ⊕ χ_B (mod 2 addition).
reasoning_steps: Find elements in A not in B: {1,2,3}.
Find elements in B not in A: {6,7,8}.
Combine these two sets to get symmetric difference.
misconceptions: Some think A Δ B = A ∪ B, which includes intersection (4,5) incorrectly.
Some think A Δ B = A ∩ B, which gives only common elements.

glossary_entry.definition: Elements belonging to exactly one of A or B.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you compute A Δ B?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Take (A−B) ∪ (B−A). This gives elements in exactly one set.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set of elements in A or B but not both.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students that 4 and 5 are excluded because they are in both A and B.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compute symmetric difference given two sets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. A−B={1,2,3}, B−A={6,7,8}, union={1,2,3,6,7,8}. Option A is intersection, B is union, D is repeated union.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The Cartesian product A × B is the set of all ordered pairs (a, b) where a ∈ A and b ∈ B. If A = {1,2} and B = {x,y}, what is A × B?
questions.hint: A × B pairs each element of A with each element of B.
questions.explanation: A × B = {(a,b) | a∈A, b∈B}. With A={1,2} and B={x,y}, all ordered pairs are: (1,x), (1,y), (2,x), (2,y). Option B lists all four correctly. Option A is incomplete, C is not ordered pairs, D pairs each element with itself.

explanations_tiered.definition: A × B = {(a, b) | a ∈ A ∧ b ∈ B}. All ordered pairs with first coordinate from A, second from B.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For |A|=2 and |B|=2, there are 2×2 = 4 ordered pairs: all combinations of first from {1,2} and second from {x,y}.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Match every first element with every second element. 1 pairs with x and y; 2 pairs with x and y. That gives 4 pairs.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Cardinality of Cartesian product: |A × B| = |A|·|B|. The set of ordered pairs forms a rectangular grid of size |A| by |B|.
worked_solution: Take a=1, pair with x and y: (1,x),(1,y)
Take a=2, pair with x and y: (2,x),(2,y)
reasoning_steps: For each element in A, pair it with every element in B.
List all resulting ordered pairs.
Count = |A| × |B| = 2 × 2 = 4 pairs.
misconceptions: Some think Cartesian product is commutative: A×B ≠ B×A generally.
Some think ordered pairs can be written as (a,b) = (b,a), which is false unless a=b.

glossary_entry.definition: The set of all ordered pairs (a, b) where a ∈ A and b ∈ B, denoted A × B.

flashcard.front: How many elements are in A × B if |A|=2 and |B|=3?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the Cartesian product A × B?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: All ordered pairs (a,b) with a∈A and b∈B.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set of all ordered pairs (a, b) where a ∈ A and b ∈ B.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that (1,x) and (x,1) are different pairs.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define and compute Cartesian product of two sets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A×B = {(1,x),(1,y),(2,x),(2,y)}. Option A is incomplete, C is not pairs, D pairs elements with themselves.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If |A| = 5 and |B| = 3, what is |A × B|?
questions.hint: The number of ordered pairs in A × B is |A| × |B|.
questions.explanation: The cardinality of the Cartesian product A × B is the product of the cardinalities: |A × B| = |A| × |B| = 5 × 3 = 15. Option B is correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: If A = {2,4,6} and B = {1,3} then A×B has 3×2=6 elements

explanations_tiered.definition: |A × B| = |A|·|B|. The number of ordered pairs equals the product of the set sizes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each of the 5 elements in A can pair with each of the 3 elements in B, giving 5 × 3 = 15 ordered pairs.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pick any of the 5 first elements. For each, you can pick any of the 3 second elements. That's 5 choices times 3 choices = 15.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Multiplication principle: |A × B| = |A| × |B|. For |A|=5, |B|=3, the product is 15.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Cartesian product is a combinatorial product. In set theory, |A × B| = |A|·|B| holds for all finite sets, and generalises to infinite cardinals.
reasoning_steps: Recall formula: |A × B| = |A| × |B|.
Substitute given values: 5 × 3.
Compute: 15.

formula_analysis.name: Cardinality of Cartesian product
formula_analysis.derivation: Multiplication principle: |A| choices for first coordinate, |B| for second.

misconceptions: Some think |A × B| = |A| + |B| (addition, not multiplication).
Some think |A × B| = |A|^|B| or |B|^|A|, which are incorrect.

glossary_entry.definition: The number of ordered pairs in A × B equals the product of the cardinalities of A and B.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the formula for |A × B|?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The number of ordered pairs in A × B is the product of the sizes of A and B.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use grid visualisation: 5 rows × 3 columns = 15 cells.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate the cardinality of Cartesian product
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. |A × B| = |A| × |B| = 5 × 3 = 15. Options A, C, D are addition or individual sizes.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If n(A) = 20, n(B) = 30, and n(A ∩ B) = 10, what is n(A ∪ B)?
questions.hint: Use the formula n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A ∩ B).
questions.explanation: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A ∩ B) = 20 + 30 − 10 = 40. Option B is correct. Option A is the sum without subtracting the intersection, C is sum plus intersection, D is n(B) alone.
formula_used: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)

calculation_steps: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)

source_trace.content_anchor: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)

explanations_tiered.definition: The inclusion-exclusion principle for two sets: n(A∪B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A∩B).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Add n(A) and n(B), then subtract n(A∩B) because elements in the intersection were counted twice.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Add the sizes of A and B, then subtract the overlap (since it was counted twice). 20+30=50, minus 10 = 40.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: n(A∪B) = 20 + 30 − 10 = 40. The intersection of 10 elements is counted in both n(A) and n(B), so it must be subtracted once.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Inclusion-exclusion principle: |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| − |A ∩ B|. This generalises to more sets with alternating sums.
reasoning_steps: Recall formula: n(A∪B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A∩B).
Substitute: 20 + 30 − 10.
Compute: 50 − 10 = 40.

formula_analysis.formula: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
formula_analysis.name: Inclusion-exclusion for two sets
formula_analysis.derivation: Elements in A∩B counted twice in n(A)+n(B), so subtract once.

misconceptions: Some forget to subtract the intersection, giving n(A)+n(B) = 50 (Option A).
Some add the intersection instead of subtracting, giving 20+30+10=60 (Option C).

glossary_entry.definition: The number of elements in the union of two sets equals the sum of their sizes minus the size of their intersection.

flashcard.front: What is n(A∪B) if n(A)=20, n(B)=30, n(A∩B)=10?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Formula for the cardinality of the union of two sets, accounting for double-counted intersection.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Venn diagram to show why intersection must be subtracted once.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply inclusion-exclusion to find union cardinality
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. n(A∪B) = 20 + 30 − 10 = 40. Option A is sum without subtraction, C is sum plus intersection, D is n(B) alone.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a survey of 120 students, 70 like mathematics, 50 like science, and 20 like both. How many students like at least one of the two subjects?
questions.hint: n(M ∪ S) = n(M) + n(S) − n(M ∩ S).
questions.explanation: Using inclusion-exclusion: n(M ∪ S) = 70 + 50 − 20 = 100. Option B is correct. Option A is total students surveyed, C is M only, D is sum without subtracting.
formula_used: n(M ∪ S) = n(M) + n(S) - n(M ∩ S)

calculation_steps: n(M ∪ S) = n(M) + n(S) - n(M ∩ S)

source_trace.content_anchor: In a survey of 200 students... how many are members of either clubs

explanations_tiered.definition: Students who like at least one subject = n(M ∪ S) = n(M) + n(S) − n(M∩S).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Add mathematics likers (70) and science likers (50), then subtract the 20 who like both to avoid double counting.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 70 like maths, 50 like science, but 20 are counted twice. So total = 70+50−20 = 100.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: n(M∪S) = 70 + 50 − 20 = 100. The 20 students who like both are included in both 70 and 50, so they must be subtracted once.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Inclusion-exclusion principle: |M ∪ S| = |M| + |S| − |M ∩ S|. Here, 100 students like at least one subject; the remaining 20 like neither.
reasoning_steps: Identify sets: M = likes mathematics, S = likes science.
At least one means M ∪ S.
Apply inclusion-exclusion: n(M∪S) = n(M) + n(S) − n(M∩S).

formula_analysis.formula: n(M ∪ S) = n(M) + n(S) - n(M ∩ S)
formula_analysis.name: Inclusion-exclusion

misconceptions: Some forget to subtract the intersection and answer 120 (sum = 120).
Some add the intersection (70+50+20=140) incorrectly.

glossary_entry.definition: Finding the number of elements in the union of two overlapping sets using inclusion-exclusion.

flashcard.front: If 70 like maths, 50 like science, 20 like both, how many like at least one?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you find how many like at least one of two subjects?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A practical application of set operations to count people in overlapping categories.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw Venn diagram: 50 in maths only, 30 in science only, 20 in both, 20 in neither.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. n(M∪S) = 70 + 50 − 20 = 100. Option A is total surveyed, C is maths only, D is sum without subtraction.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If n(A ∪ B) = 36, n(A) = 20, and n(B) = 28, what is n(A ∩ B)?
questions.hint: Rearrange n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A ∩ B) to solve for n(A ∩ B).
questions.explanation: n(A ∩ B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A ∪ B) = 20 + 28 − 36 = 12. Option A is correct. Option B is 8, C is 16, D is n(A) itself.
formula_used: n(A ∩ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∪ B)

calculation_steps: n(A ∩ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∪ B)

explanations_tiered.definition: Rearranging inclusion-exclusion gives n(A∩B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A∪B).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Substitute given values: 20 + 28 − 36 = 12. The intersection size is 12.
explanations_tiered.beginner: We know total in A and B together is 36. A has 20, B has 28. Adding gives 48, but 48−36=12 must be the overlap (counted twice).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From n(A∪B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A∩B), we get n(A∩B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A∪B) = 48 − 36 = 12.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Inclusion-exclusion rearrangement allows solving for intersection when union and individual sizes are known. This is useful in survey problems where overlap is unknown.
reasoning_steps: Start with formula: n(A∪B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A∩B).
Rearrange to isolate n(A∩B): n(A∩B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A∪B).
Substitute: 20 + 28 − 36.
Compute: 48 − 36 = 12.

formula_analysis.formula: n(A ∩ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∪ B)
formula_analysis.name: Intersection from union

misconceptions: Some add n(A∪B) instead of subtracting, giving 20+28+36=84.
Some do n(A∪B) − n(A) − n(B) = 36−20−28 = −12, forgetting absolute value.

glossary_entry.definition: The size of the intersection can be found using inclusion-exclusion when union and individual sizes are known.

flashcard.front: How do you find n(A∩B) if you know n(A), n(B), and n(A∪B)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: If n(A)=20, n(B)=28, n(A∪B)=36, what is n(A∩B)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Using inclusion-exclusion to find the size of the intersection from union and individual sizes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: This is a common exam question type: given union and individual sizes, find overlap.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply inclusion-exclusion to find intersection cardinality

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a group of 60 people, 35 drink tea, 25 drink coffee, and 15 drink both. How many drink only tea?
questions.explanation: Only tea means those who drink tea but not coffee: n(T − C) = n(T) − n(T ∩ C) = 35 − 15 = 20. Option B is correct. Option A is total tea drinkers, C is both, D is only coffee.

source_trace.content_anchor: How many students are only in Nature club

explanations_tiered.definition: Only tea = n(T − C) = n(T) − n(T ∩ C).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Subtract those who drink both from total tea drinkers. 35 total tea drinkers minus 15 who also drink coffee gives 20 who drink only tea.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 35 people drink tea. 15 of them also drink coffee, so the ones who drink only tea are 35 minus 15 = 20.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: n(only T) = n(T) − n(T∩C) = 35 − 15 = 20. Similarly, only coffee = 25 − 15 = 10.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Only tea corresponds to the set difference T − C. Its cardinality is n(T) − n(T∩C) because T∩C ⊆ T for finite sets.
reasoning_steps: Identify total tea drinkers: 35.
Identify tea drinkers who also drink coffee: 15.
Subtract both from total tea to get only tea: 35 − 15 = 20.
misconceptions: Some answer total tea drinkers (35) without subtracting both.
Some subtract from coffee instead (25−15=10), giving only coffee.

glossary_entry.definition: Elements belonging to A but not to B, denoted A − B or 'only A'.

flashcard.front: How do you find the number who drink only tea?
flashcard.back: Only tea = total tea drinkers − those who drink both tea and coffee.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: If 35 drink tea, 25 coffee, 15 both, how many drink only tea?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Elements that belong to A but not to B; cardinality = n(A) − n(A∩B).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw Venn diagram: tea-only region = total tea minus overlap.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate the number in exactly one of two overlapping sets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Only tea = n(Tea) − n(Both) = 35 − 15 = 20. Option A is total tea, C is both, D is only coffee.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a class of 40 students, 25 play football, 20 play basketball, and 10 play both. How many play neither sport?
questions.hint: Neither = total students − n(F ∪ B). Find union first.
questions.explanation: n(F ∪ B) = 25 + 20 − 10 = 35. Neither = total − union = 40 − 35 = 5. Option A is correct. Option B is both, C is only football, D is only basketball.
formula_used: n(F ∪ B) = n(F) + n(B) - n(F ∩ B)

source_trace.content_anchor: How many students are not members of both clubs

explanations_tiered.definition: Neither = total − n(F ∪ B). First find union via inclusion-exclusion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: n(F ∪ B) = 25 + 20 − 10 = 35. Subtract from total (40) to find neither: 5.
explanations_tiered.beginner: First find how many play at least one sport: 25+20−10=35. Then subtract from class total: 40−35=5 who play none.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: n(F ∪ B) = 35. The complement of the union gives those in neither: n((F ∪ B)′) = total − n(F ∪ B) = 40 − 35 = 5.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Neither corresponds to (F ∪ B)′ = F′ ∩ B′. Its cardinality is |U| − |F ∪ B| = 40 − 35 = 5.
reasoning_steps: Find number playing at least one sport: n(F∪B) = n(F)+n(B)−n(F∩B).
Subtract from total students to find neither: 40 − 35 = 5.

formula_analysis.name: Neither formula

variables.meaning: Number who play at least one sport

formula_analysis.derivation: Complement of union gives elements in neither set.

misconceptions: Some answer the intersection (10) as neither.
Some do total − n(F) − n(B) = 40−25−20 = −5, missing the addition back of intersection.

glossary_entry.definition: Elements belonging to neither A nor B; cardinality = |U| − |A ∪ B|.

flashcard.front: How do you find how many play neither sport?
flashcard.back: Find n(F∪B) first, then subtract from total: neither = total − n(F∪B).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you find the number who play neither of two sports?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Neither = total − n(A∪B). Find union first using inclusion-exclusion.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Elements not in A and not in B; equals (A ∪ B)′.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw Venn diagram with universal box; neither is outside both circles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate the number in neither of two overlapping sets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. n(F∪B)=25+20−10=35, neither=40−35=5. Option B is both, C is only football, D is only basketball.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a survey of 200 students, 90 are in Nature club, 31 are in Mini-media club, and 21 are in both. How many are in neither club?
questions.explanation: n(N ∪ M) = 90 + 31 − 21 = 100. Neither = 200 − 100 = 100. Option A is correct. Option B is n(M) alone, C is n(N) alone, D is n(N) + n(M).
formula_used: n(N ∪ M) = n(N) + n(M) - n(N ∩ M)

source_trace.content_anchor: In a survey of 200 students, 90 are members of Nature club, 31 are members of Mini-media club, 21 are members of both clubs. How many students are not members of both clubs.

explanations_tiered.definition: Neither = total − n(N ∪ M). Find n(N ∪ M) using inclusion-exclusion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Add Nature (90) and Mini-media (31), subtract overlap (21) to get 100 in at least one club. Subtract from total 200 to get 100 in neither.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: n(N ∪ M) = 100. The complement (N ∪ M)′ has cardinality |U| − |N ∪ M| = 200 − 100 = 100.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This problem demonstrates the complement of union: neither = |U| − (|N| + |M| − |N ∩ M|). The result is exactly half the surveyed students.
reasoning_steps: Compute union: n(N∪M) = n(N) + n(M) − n(N∩M).
Substitute: 90 + 31 − 21 = 100.
Neither = total students − union = 200 − 100 = 100.

formula_analysis.formula: n(N ∪ M) = n(N) + n(M) - n(N ∩ M)
formula_analysis.name: Inclusion-exclusion

misconceptions: Some answer 200 − 90 − 31 = 79, forgetting to add back the intersection (Option B).
Some answer just 90 (Nature) or 31 (Mini-media) as neither.

glossary_entry.definition: Number of respondents who are in neither of two categories; complement of the union.

flashcard.front: In a survey of 200 students, 90 in Nature, 31 in Mini-media, 21 in both. How many in neither?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you find the number in neither of two clubs from survey data?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Number of respondents who belong to neither of two given categories.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: This exact problem appears as Exercise 1.10 in the PDF.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve two-set survey problems using inclusion-exclusion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. n(N∪M)=90+31−21=100, neither=200−100=100. Option B (79) is total − n(N) − n(M), C is n(N), D is n(N)+n(M).
